1: The Spirit transports Ezekiel to the east gate of the temple. The theme of Spirit-mediated transportation runs through Ezekiel (Ezekiel 3:12; Ezekiel 8:3; Ezekiel 43:5) and points toward Christ, in whom the Spirit dwells without measure (John 3:34). The east gate will be the site of the glory's departure (Ezekiel 11:22-23) and, in the vision of restoration, sealed for YHWH's return (Ezekiel 44:2).
2: The men plotting harm and giving wicked counsel in the city illustrate the corruption of leadership that Ezekiel addresses throughout. The revelation of God here is that YHWH sees what is hidden — the hearts of those who counsel violence. Christ's knowledge of the human heart (John 2:25) fulfills this omniscient disclosure: the divine scrutiny that condemned Jerusalem's counselors is the same scrutiny that searches every human heart.
3: The proverb "Let us build houses — this city is the caldron, and we are the flesh" was a claim of security: those inside the city are safe, slow-cooked to strength. The ironic reversal in Ezekiel 11:7 — the slain are the flesh in the caldron — exposes false security built on violence. The theme connects to Christ's teaching that those who seek to save their life will lose it (Matthew 16:25).
4: The prophetic commission — "prophesy against them" — reveals the cost of the prophetic office. Every true prophet stands between YHWH and a people bent on self-destruction. Christ inherits and completes this pattern: the ultimate prophet who speaks the word of judgment against his own people and dies for it (Luke 13:33-34).
5: The Spirit of the LORD comes upon Ezekiel and authorizes his speech. A type: the Spirit's descent on a prophet, enabling authoritative proclamation of divine words, anticipates Christ's baptismal anointing (Isaiah 61:1; Luke 4:18). The critical difference is degree and permanence — the Spirit rested on OT prophets for specific moments; on Christ the Spirit descends and remains (John 1:32-33).
6: The city filled with the slain — the violence perpetrated by Jerusalem's leaders is catalogued. The revelation of God is that YHWH holds rulers accountable for bloodshed. Christ absorbs this divine accountability for violence; on the cross he bears the guilt of every violent act that the prophets condemned.
7: The ironic reversal: those who claimed the city was a caldron protecting them find that the slain — the victims of their violence — are the flesh inside, while they will be driven out. The revelation of God inverts human power assumptions. Christ enacts the ultimate inversion: the powerful are scattered; the victims are vindicated.
8: "I will bring the sword upon you" — the judgment the leaders feared is brought not by chance but by YHWH's sovereign decision. The revelation of God's justice operates through historical agency (Babylon) as instrument. Christ's passion narrative follows the same pattern: the judgment YHWH brings is absorbed by Christ himself — he who knew no violence bears the sword that justice demands.
9: Being handed over to foreigners and executed at the border — the language of exile and judgment. A theme that directly anticipates Christ: Jesus is handed over to the Gentiles (the Romans), executed outside the city walls (Hebrews 13:12). The exile-judgment pattern of Ezekiel 11 maps onto Christ's passion with structural precision.
10: "You will know that I am the LORD" — the recognition formula that punctuates judgment oracles throughout Ezekiel. The revelation of God: every act of judgment is simultaneously a self-disclosure. Christ's cross is the definitive event in which YHWH's identity is revealed — the God who judges sin by absorbing it in the Son (2 Corinthians 5:21).
11: The city will not be the exiles' caldron — the false security is dismantled. Judgment happens at the border, outside the presumed zone of safety. The revelation of God: no institutional, geographic, or ethnic privilege secures against judgment. Christ dismantles all such presumption (Matthew 3:9) and offers instead a security grounded in union with himself.
12: The covenant failure — not walking in YHWH's statutes, acting according to the nations' judgments instead. The theme of covenant breach that the entire law system proved insufficient to prevent. Christ's perfect covenantal obedience (the active righteousness credited to believers, Philippians 3:9) is the answer to what 11:12 diagnoses.
13: Pelatiah dies during Ezekiel's prophesying. The prophetic word carries immediate, death-dealing force. This reveals the seriousness of the prophetic office and the power of YHWH's word. Christ's words are described with the same gravitas — sharper than any two-edged sword (Hebrews 4:12); the word that judges the living and the dead (2 Timothy 4:1).
14: A brief narrative transition — "the word of the LORD came to me again" — before the great promise of Ezekiel 11:15-21. These formulaic transitions mark the rhythm of prophetic reception. In Christ, the one who receives and mediates the word becomes the Word itself (John 1:1). The messenger and the message are finally identical.
15: The inhabitants of Jerusalem despise the exiles, claiming the land belongs to those who remained. YHWH overturns this: the exiles, not the self-satisfied in Jerusalem, are his true people. The revelation of God vindicates the humiliated and scattered. Christ enacts this pattern precisely — rejected by the Jerusalem establishment, he is the one in whom YHWH's covenant people are reconstituted.
16: A direct type: "Although I sent them far away among the nations and scattered them among the countries, yet I have been a little sanctuary (miqdash me'at) to them in the countries where they have gone." YHWH himself becomes the sanctuary for the exiles in a land without temple. This is the pattern Christ fulfills in two directions: in his incarnation he is Immanuel — God with his scattered people wherever they are; and in John 2:19-21 Jesus identifies his own body as the temple, making himself the permanent, portable sanctuary that replaces the one Ezekiel watched depart. The dwelling of God with humanity (Revelation 21:3) is the eschatological completion of what 11:16 promises.
17: The promise of regathering from the nations. A shadow: the eschatological ingathering that Jesus initiates. "I will gather my elect from the four winds" (Matthew 24:31) is the Christological enactment of Ezekiel's regathering oracle. The scattered people of God, reconstituted in Christ, are the fulfillment of what YHWH promises here.
18: When they return, they will remove all detestable things and abominations. The restoration leads to purification. A shadow of the new creation's purity: "nothing unclean will enter" the new Jerusalem (Revelation 21:27). Christ's atoning work provides the basis on which YHWH can dwell with a purified people — the abominations are removed because the one who bore them (Isaiah 53:11) has carried them away.
19: A direct shadow: "I will give them one heart (lev echad) and put a new spirit within them; I will remove the heart of stone from their flesh and give them a heart of flesh." The New Covenant promise, given in fuller form in Ezekiel 36:26-27 and Jeremiah 31:31-34, is enacted by Christ's death and sealed by the Spirit at Pentecost (Acts 2). Paul's description of the Christian life — "you are a letter from Christ... written not with ink but with the Spirit of the living God, not on tablets of stone but on tablets of human hearts" (2 Corinthians 3:3) — is 11:19 in Christological fulfillment.
20: The covenant formula: "They will be my people, and I will be their God." A direct shadow: this is the covenant formula that runs through Scripture (Genesis 17:7; Exodus 6:7; Jeremiah 31:33) and reaches its eschatological fulfillment in Christ (2 Corinthians 6:16; Revelation 21:3). Jesus's high-priestly prayer — "that they may be one" and "that they may be with me where I am" (John 17:21-24) — is the Christological enactment of this covenant formula.
21: Those whose hearts pursue detestable idols will bear their own conduct on their heads. The revelation of God: covenant judgment falls on those who reject the covenant renewal offered. The warning anticipates Hebrews' language about those who "trample the Son of God underfoot" (Hebrews 10:29) — the greater the revelation, the greater the accountability for rejecting it.
22: The cherubim spread their wings and the glory of the God of Israel rises above them. The glory is in motion — departing. A shadow: the story of God's glory is not static; it departs in judgment and returns in redemption. Christ is the one in whom the glory returns permanently: "the Word became flesh and tabernacled among us, and we have seen his glory" (John 1:14).
23: A direct shadow: "The glory of the LORD rose up from within the city and stood on the mountain to the east of the city" — the Mount of Olives. This is the same mountain from which Jesus ascends in Acts 1:9-12, and to which the angels promise he will return (Acts 1:11; Zechariah 14:4). Ezekiel's vision of the glory resting on the eastern mountain frames the Christological narrative: the glory departs from a people who have rejected YHWH; the glory returns in Christ's incarnation; the final return is his Parousia from the east. The departure and the promise of return belong to the same canonical arc.
24: The Spirit brings Ezekiel back in vision to the exiles in Chaldea. The prophet's experience — transported by the Spirit, shown the severity of Jerusalem's judgment, carried back to share it — mirrors the structure of all prophetic ministry. Christ himself is the one who descends from the Father, is "lifted up" in crucifixion and ascension, and sends the Spirit to those scattered throughout the world.
25: Ezekiel reports to the exiles everything YHWH had shown him. The prophetic task is faithful testimony to what has been received in vision. Christ is simultaneously the sender, the vision, and the one reported: the apostolic proclamation (Acts 1:8) enacts 11:25 on an eschatological scale — witnesses carried to the ends of the earth by the Spirit to announce what they have seen.
